relationship_corrections.reason: Composition (many left, one right) is incorrect; an Actor has many Permissions, so undirected association with one-to-many is appropriate. Id 1 already shows the correct association.
Dependency with cardinality is misplaced; many AlternativeRunways can accommodate many AircraftTypes, so many-to-many association is more accurate.
One-to-one is too restrictive; a closed runway can have many alternative runways, so many alternative runways associated with one runway (a runway has many alternatives).
Each flight has exactly one active slot at a time; the existing zero-to-many multiplicity contradicts the one-to-one relationship in id 10. Corrected to one-to-one.
A critical flight is a subtype of Flight, not an associated entity. Swapped classes and used inheritance to reflect that CriticalFlight extends Flight.
Each flight owns one emergency state marker as part of its lifecycle; one-to-one composition (Flight contains EmergencyStateMarker) is more appropriate than association.
The direct Flight-Slot association (id 10) already models the allocation; FlightAssignment should only depend on TimeSlot, not have a separate association, to avoid redundancy.
An Operation uses an Interface, but composition implies ownership; directed association is more appropriate for a usage relationship.
A Resource is not composed of Actors; many Actors can use many Resources, so many-to-many association is correct.
Composition is incorrect; a simple one-to-one association between Resource and Actor (e.g., a resource currently assigned to one actor) is more plausible.
Each slot is assigned to exactly one runway; the zero-or-one multiplicity on Slot side is incorrect—a slot cannot exist without a runway.

architecture_corrections.description: FlightDataStore is declared as an interface but is used as a concrete data store for Flight entities.
RunwaySlotDB is declared as an interface but is used as a concrete database for runway slot data.
TimetableUI is declared as an interface but represents a concrete user interface component.
CriticalFlight inherits from Flight, but the domain describes emergency as a state of a flight, not a separate entity.

details.reason: Emergency should be a state/attribute of Flight, not a subclass.

architecture_corrections.description: FlightAssignment should have a direct association with Flight, not just a dependency on TimeSlot.
Runway should have a direct association with TimeSlot to support slot allocation per runway.
Slot and TimeSlot appear to represent the same concept (a time slot for a flight). They should be merged.
EmergencyStateMarker is a composition inside Flight, but emergency is a state that should be an attribute or enum, not a separate class.

details.reason: Emergency state should be modeled as an attribute or enum value of Flight.

architecture_suggestions.suggestion: Consider introducing an aggregate root 'Schedule' that manages the allocation of TimeSlots to Flights on Runways, encapsulating the slot allocation and gap enforcement logic.
Introduce a value object 'FlightNumber' to encapsulate the flight number format and validation, rather than using a primitive string.
Rename 'AlternativeRunway' to 'RunwayAssignment' or 'RunwayReassignment' to better reflect its role in handling runway closures.
The 'Operation' class appears to be a generic permission/resource wrapper. Consider splitting it into more specific domain concepts like 'FlightOperation' and 'RunwayOperation'.
Extract a common base interface 'Repository' for FlightDataStore and RunwaySlotDB to unify data access patterns.
